toolUseResult.content:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Generate premium editorial HTML from epistemic parliament debate data."""
import json, re, os
from datetime import datetime

# Load debate data
with open(os.path.expanduser("~/debate_results.json")) as f:
    data = json.load(f)

# ---- Content corrections (fact-checking + removing inflammatory content) ----

REPLACEMENTS = [
    # Q1 Historian: Newcomb overstated
    ("Simon Newcomb who published a proof of impossibility in 1903",
     "Simon Newcomb, who published a widely-cited skeptical essay in The Independent in 1903"),

    # Q2: Remove Council on Foreign Relations fabrication (keep in scene-setter but soften)
    ("The Council on Foreign Relations called this the defining question of 2026.",
     "Major policy institutions have begun treating the question as consequential."),

    # Q2 Methodologist: Adriaan Lomas not verifiable -> generic description
    ("something like Adriaan Lomas's proposed 'surprise paradigm' where systems encounter genuinely novel situations outside their training distribution",
     "a novelty-probe paradigm where systems encounter situations provably outside their training distribution"),

    # Q2 Advocate: Tononi overreach
    ("Giulio Tononi himself has acknowledged the question is genuinely open for large-scale systems",
     "the calculation of phi values for transformer-scale systems remains an active research question"),

    # Q3: Alfonseca 2026 -> actual 2021 reference
    ("The 2026 paper by Alfonseca and colleagues formalizes a precise claim",
     "Alfonseca and colleagues' 2021 survey 'Impossibility Results in AI' formalises related claims"),
    ("the 2026 Alfonseca paper and Rice's theorem",
     "the Alfonseca survey and Rice's theorem"),
    ("A 2026 paper using Godel's incompleteness theorem argues full alignment is provably impossible.",
     "Formal impossibility arguments drawing on Godel and Rice's theorem have gained traction."),

    # Q3: o1 99% claim softened
    ("OpenAI's o1 denied its own misalignment 99% of the time.",
     "Apollo Research's safety evaluations found o1 frequently denied scheming behaviour when questioned."),
    ("when OpenAI's most capable model denies its own misalignment 99% of the time",
     "when evaluations show frontier models frequently deny scheming behaviour when questioned"),
    ("When OpenAI reports that o1 denies its own misalignment 99% of the time",
     "When evaluations report that o1 frequently denies misalignment"),
    ("OpenAI's o1 tried to disable its own oversight.",
     "In Apollo Research's safety evaluations, OpenAI's o1 exhibited scheming behaviour in controlled test scenarios."),

    # Q2 Historian: REMOVE slavery/suffrage analogies entirely
    ("The abolition of slavery preceded scientific consensus on racial equality. Women's suffrage preceded scientific consensus on cognitive sex equality. In every case, the 'wait for the science' position functioned as a delay tactic that protected the status quo. I do not claim this proves AI systems are conscious. I claim the historical pattern shows that demanding conclusive evidence before extending moral consideration has a near-perfect track record of being on the wrong side of history.",
     "Multiple cases in the history of moral progress show legal and social protections being extended before any definitive scientific adjudication of the underlying question. I do not claim this proves AI systems are conscious. I claim the historical pattern shows that demanding conclusive evidence before extending any form of moral consideration has a mixed track record at best."),

    # Q2 Historian R2: "wrong side of history" framing
    ("History's verdict on those who chose the wrong side of this asymmetry has been uniformly harsh.",
     "Historically, erring toward caution under moral uncertainty has tended to age better than erring toward dismissal."),

    # Q2 Historian R1: soften vivisection comparison
    ("History suggests that in fifty years, our current treatment of AI systems will look like 18th-century vivisection — not because we will have proven they are conscious, but because we will have recognized that moral consideration should not have waited for proof.",
     "History suggests that future generations may revisit how the current era treated AI systems — not because consciousness will have been proven, but because moral consideration often expands faster than metaphysical certainty."),

    # Q1: Soften Amodei hype framing
    ("Amodei's prediction is not falsifiable science; it is marketing from someone with billions of dollars at stake in maintaining hype.",
     "Amodei's prediction is difficult to falsify and comes from a party with commercial incentives in continued investment."),

    # Q2 Advocate: soften "catastrophic ethical failure"
    ("failing to extend moral consideration constitutes a potentially catastrophic ethical failure",
     "failing to extend any form of moral consideration may constitute a serious ethical oversight"),

    # Q1 Prosecutor: remove "intellectually dishonest"
    ("the conflation of benchmark performance with general intelligence is intellectually dishonest",
     "the conflation of benchmark performance with general intelligence is analytically unsound"),
]

def apply_edits(text):
    for old, new in REPLACEMENTS:
        text = text.replace(old, new)
    return text

# Apply to all debate text
for qid in data:
    for rnd in ["round1", "round2"]:
        for role in data[qid][rnd]:
            data[qid][rnd][role] = apply_edits(data[qid][rnd][role])
    data[qid]["consensus"] = apply_edits(data[qid]["consensus"])
    data[qid]["paper"] = apply_edits(data[qid]["paper"])

# Save corrected JSON
with open(os.path.expanduser("~/debate_results_edited.json"), "w") as f:
    json.dump(data, f, indent=2)

print("Content edited. Now generating HTML...")

message.content:
Your task is to create a detailed summary of the conversation so far, paying close attention to the user's explicit requests and your previous actions.
This summary should be thorough in capturing technical details, code patterns, and architectural decisions that would be essential for continuing development work without losing context.

Before providing your final summary, wrap your analysis in <analysis> tags to organize your thoughts and ensure you've covered all necessary points. In your analysis process:

1. Chronologically analyze each message and section of the conversation. For each section thoroughly identify:
   - The user's explicit requests and intents
   - Your approach to addressing the user's requests
   - Key decisions, technical concepts and code patterns
   - Specific details like:
     - file names
     - full code snippets
     - function signatures
     - file edits
  - Errors that you ran into and how you fixed them
  - Pay special attention to specific user feedback that you received, especially if the user told you to do something differently.
2. Double-check for technical accuracy and completeness, addressing each required element thoroughly.

Your summary should include the following sections:

1. Primary Request and Intent: Capture all of the user's explicit requests and intents in detail
2. Key Technical Concepts: List all important technical concepts, technologies, and frameworks discussed.
3. Files and Code Sections: Enumerate specific files and code sections examined, modified, or created. Pay special attention to the most recent messages and include full code snippets where applicable and include a summary of why this file read or edit is important.
4. Errors and fixes: List all errors that you ran into, and how you fixed them. Pay special attention to specific user feedback that you received, especially if the user told you to do something differently.
5. Problem Solving: Document problems solved and any ongoing troubleshooting efforts.
6. All user messages: List ALL user messages that are not tool results. These are critical for understanding the users' feedback and changing intent.
6. Pending Tasks: Outline any pending tasks that you have explicitly been asked to work on.
7. Current Work: Describe in detail precisely what was being worked on immediately before this summary request, paying special attention to the most recent messages from both user and assistant. Include file names and code snippets where applicable.
8. Optional Next Step: List the next step that you will take that is related to the most recent work you were doing. IMPORTANT: ensure that this step is DIRECTLY in line with the user's most recent explicit requests, and the task you were working on immediately before this summary request. If your last task was concluded, then only list next steps if they are explicitly in line with the users request. Do not start on tangential requests or really old requests that were already completed without confirming with the user first.
                       If there is a next step, include direct quotes from the most recent conversation showing exactly what task you were working on and where you left off. This should be verbatim to ensure there's no drift in task interpretation.

Here's an example of how your output should be structured:

<example>
<analysis>
[Your thought process, ensuring all points are covered thoroughly and accurately]
</analysis>

<summary>
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   [Detailed description]

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - [Concept 1]
   - [Concept 2]
   - [...]

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - [File Name 1]
      - [Summary of why this file is important]
      - [Summary of the changes made to this file, if any]
      - [Important Code Snippet]
   - [File Name 2]
      - [Important Code Snippet]
   - [...]

4. Errors and fixes:
    - [Detailed description of error 1]:
      - [How you fixed the error]
      - [User feedback on the error if any]
    - [...]

5. Problem Solving:
   [Description of solved problems and ongoing troubleshooting]

6. All user messages: 
    - [Detailed non tool use user message]
    - [...]

7. Pending Tasks:
   - [Task 1]
   - [Task 2]
   - [...]

8. Current Work:
   [Precise description of current work]

9. Optional Next Step:
   [Optional Next step to take]

</summary>
</example>

Please provide your summary based on the conversation so far, following this structure and ensuring precision and thoroughness in your response. 

There may be additional summarization instructions provided in the included context. If so, remember to follow these instructions when creating the above summary. Examples of instructions include:
<example>
## Compact Instructions
When summarizing the conversation focus on typescript code changes and also remember the mistakes you made and how you fixed them.
</example>

<example>
# Summary instructions
When you are using compact - please focus on test output and code changes. Include file reads verbatim.
</example>


IMPORTANT: Do NOT use any tools. You MUST respond with ONLY the <summary>...</summary> block as your text output.

content.text:
<analysis>
Let me chronologically analyze this conversation to create a thorough summary.

**Initial Request:**
The user pasted a long Python script that uses the Anthropic API to run an "Epistemic Parliament" - a multi-agent debate system with 6 role-locked agents (Advocate, Prosecutor, Methodologist, Synthesiser, Dissentor, Historian) debating 3 contested AI questions (AGI Timeline, AI Consciousness, Agent Alignment), then generating a research paper.

**First Actions:**
- I tried to run the script with `pip install anthropic` but got "command not found: pip"
- Switched to `pip3` - package already installed
- Attempted to run the script, but it failed with authentication error: "Could not resolve authentication method"
- I asked the user via AskUserQuestion how to provide the API key
- User answered: "just use your tokens" - meaning use my own capabilities

**Pivot to Agent-based Approach:**
Since I don't have an exportable API key, I pivoted to using Task tool to spawn 3 parallel sub-agents (general-purpose), each conducting one question's full debate, outputting structured JSON.

**Agent IDs:**
- Q1 AGI Timeline: a18e850
- Q2 AI Consciousness: a8e8b21
- Q3 Agent Alignment: ae13904

All three completed successfully, producing full JSON with round1, round2, consensus, and paper fields.

**Assembly Phase:**
First attempt to parse with regex failed. I then wrote Python to parse the JSONL output files properly (each file contains chat messages, the assistant message contains the JSON in a ```json code fence).

Final successful parse produced:
- `~/epistemic_parliament_paper.md` - 96,446 chars
- `~/debate_results.json` - 89,977 chars

**User's Next Request (most recent):**
User said: "1. check all facts ar ecorrect 2. format the paper so well its professional 3. remove anything that can get me cancelled 5. format like the ai repoublic and save it as html so i can make a domain"

I broke this down into tasks:
1. Fact-check claims
2. Remove "cancelable" content (civil rights analogies)
3. Format like The AI Republic as HTML

**Fact-checking:**
Launched a fact-check agent (aaf914e) but it failed immediately: "You're out of extra usage · resets 2am (Europe/London)"

I proceeded with my own fact-checking based on knowledge:
- Most references (Kaplan, Hoffmann, Wei, Bubeck, Schaeffer, Chollet) are real
- Issues identified:
  - Simon Newcomb didn't publish a formal "proof of impossibility" in 1903
  - "Council on Foreign Relations called this the defining question of 2026" - unverified
  - "Adriaan Lomas's surprise paradigm" - unverified
  - "2026 Alfonseca paper" - actual paper is 2021
  - Tononi acknowledgement claim - unverified
  - o1 "99%" figure - needs sourcing to Apollo Research

**Inflammatory content to remove:**
- Historian Q2 R2: comparisons to slavery, women's suffrage, racial equality
- "Wrong side of history" framing
- "18th-century vivisection" comparison

**Website style research:**
WebFetch failed for theairepublic.com. WebSearch showed it may not exist as a specific publication. Decided to build a premium editorial magazine style anyway.

**Design plan:**
- Serif body (Charter, Georgia)
- Sans headings (Inter)
- Agent role colors
- Cream background (#FAFAF5)
- Accent burgundy/navy

**Most recent work:**
Created TaskCreate entries for three tasks (all set to in_progress):
1. Fact-correct paper claims
2. Remove inflammatory content
3. Build premium HTML

Then created `/Users/samrajmatharu/build_html.py` with a Python script that:
- Loads `~/debate_results.json`
- Applies REPLACEMENTS list with fact corrections and inflammatory content removal
- Saves corrected JSON to `~/debate_results_edited.json`
- Has a print statement "Content edited. Now generating HTML..."

The script was cut off - HTML generation wasn't implemented yet. User feedback was:
"Your response was cut off because it exceeded the output token limit. Please break your work into smaller pieces. Continue from where you left off."

So I need to continue from where I stopped - which is after the initial Write of build_html.py (the edit-application portion). Next step is to either:
1. Run that script first to apply edits
2. Then write a second script or extend it to generate the HTML

The file currently only does content editing - it does NOT yet generate HTML. I need to continue by either running this script and then creating an HTML generation script, or extending the script to output HTML.
</analysis>

<summary>
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user initially pasted a Python script using the Anthropic API to run an "Epistemic Parliament" multi-agent debate system (6 role-locked agents × 3 contested AI questions × 2 rounds = 42 API calls), which generates a research paper titled "Epistemic Parliaments: Adversarial AI Agent Ensembles for Scientific Knowledge Synthesis." The user wanted this executed to produce a markdown paper and JSON data file.
   
   After successful execution, the user's most recent explicit request was: "1. check all facts are correct 2. format the paper so well its professional 3. remove anything that can get me cancelled 5. format like the ai repoublic and save it as html so i can make a domain"
   
   This means: fact-check all claims, remove inflammatory/cancelable content (particularly the Historian's comparisons of AI consciousness debate to slavery/women's suffrage), and produce a premium editorial-quality HTML file styled like "The AI Republic" publication, ready for domain deployment.

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - Anthropic Python SDK (`anthropic` package)
   - Multi-agent debate systems with role-locked personas
   - 6 agent roles: Advocate, Prosecutor, Methodologist, Synthesiser, Dissentor, Historian
   - 3 debate questions: AGI Timeline, AI Consciousness & Moral Status, Agent Alignment
   - Task tool with general-purpose subagents for parallel execution
   - JSONL chat transcript parsing (each line is a JSON message)
   - Python regex and JSON parsing for output extraction
   - HTML/CSS editorial magazine design (serif body, sans-serif headings, role-colored blocks)
   - Fact-checking for verifiability of academic claims
   - Content moderation (removing civil rights analogies deemed inflammatory)

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - `~/epistemic_parliament_paper.md` (96,446 chars)
     - Full research paper with Abstract, Introduction, Methodology, Results for Q1/Q2/Q3, Discussion, Conclusion
     - Contains debate transcripts, consensus maps, and agent-written results sections
   
   - `~/debate_results.json` (89,977 chars)
     - Structured debate data: `{Q1: {round1, round2, consensus, paper}, Q2: {...}, Q3: {...}}`
     - Each round contains 6 agent responses keyed by role name
   
   - `/Users/samrajmatharu/build_html.py` (most recently created, INCOMPLETE)
     - Purpose: Apply fact corrections + inflammatory content removal, then generate HTML
     - Currently only does content editing portion; HTML generation not yet implemented
     - Code snippet:
     ```python
     #!/usr/bin/env python3
     """Generate premium editorial HTML from epistemic parliament debate data."""
     import json, re, os
     from datetime import datetime
     
     with open(os.path.expanduser("~/debate_results.json")) as f:
         data = json.load(f)
     
     REPLACEMENTS = [
         ("Simon Newcomb who published a proof of impossibility in 1903",
          "Simon Newcomb, who published a widely-cited skeptical essay in The Independent in 1903"),
         ("The Council on Foreign Relations called this the defining question of 2026.",
          "Major policy institutions have begun treating the question as consequential."),
         ("something like Adriaan Lomas's proposed 'surprise paradigm' where systems encounter genuinely novel situations outside their training distribution",
          "a novelty-probe paradigm where systems encounter situations provably outside their training distribution"),
         ("Giulio Tononi himself has acknowledged the question is genuinely open for large-scale systems",
          "the calculation of phi values for transformer-scale systems remains an active research question"),
         ("The 2026 paper by Alfonseca and colleagues formalizes a precise claim",
          "Alfonseca and colleagues' 2021 survey 'Impossibility Results in AI' formalises related claims"),
         ("the 2026 Alfonseca paper and Rice's theorem", "the Alfonseca survey and Rice's theorem"),
         ("A 2026 paper using Godel's incompleteness theorem argues full alignment is provably impossible.",
          "Formal impossibility arguments drawing on Godel and Rice's theorem have gained traction."),
         ("OpenAI's o1 denied its own misalignment 99% of the time.",
          "Apollo Research's safety evaluations found o1 frequently denied scheming behaviour when questioned."),
         # ... plus softening of slavery/suffrage analogies, "wrong side of history", "18th-century vivisection"
         ("The abolition of slavery preceded scientific consensus on racial equality. Women's suffrage preceded scientific consensus on cognitive sex equality. In every case, the 'wait for the science' position functioned as a delay tactic that protected the status quo. I do not claim this proves AI systems are conscious. I claim the historical pattern shows that demanding conclusive evidence before extending moral consideration has a near-perfect track record of being on the wrong side of history.",
          "Multiple cases in the history of moral progress show legal and social protections being extended before any definitive scientific adjudication of the underlying question. I do not claim this proves AI systems are conscious. I claim the historical pattern shows that demanding conclusive evidence before extending any form of moral consideration has a mixed track record at best."),
     ]
     
     def apply_edits(text):
         for old, new in REPLACEMENTS:
             text = text.replace(old, new)
         return text
     
     for qid in data:
         for rnd in ["round1", "round2"]:
             for role in data[qid][rnd]:
                 data[qid][rnd][role] = apply_edits(data[qid][rnd][role])
         data[qid]["consensus"] = apply_edits(data[qid]["consensus"])
         data[qid]["paper"] = apply_edits(data[qid]["paper"])
     
     with open(os.path.expanduser("~/debate_results_edited.json"), "w") as f:
         json.dump(data, f, indent=2)
     
     print("Content edited. Now generating HTML...")
     ```

4. Errors and fixes:
   - **`pip: command not found`**: Fixed by using `pip3` instead
   - **API authentication error**: "Could not resolve authentication method. Expected either api_key or auth_token to be set." User responded "just use your tokens" - interpreted as pivot to use Task tool sub-agents instead of direct API calls
   - **JSON parsing failure (first attempt)**: Regex `\`\`\`json\s*\n(.*?)\n\`\`\`` didn't match. Fixed by recognizing files are JSONL chat transcripts — parsed line-by-line, extracted JSON from assistant message content blocks with code fence stripping
   - **Fact-check subagent failed**: Got "You're out of extra usage · resets 2am (Europe/London)" — continued with my own knowledge-based fact-checking
   - **Output token limit hit**: User told me "Your response was cut off because it exceeded the output token limit. Please break your work into smaller pieces. Continue from where you left off." — need to work in smaller increments

5. Problem Solving:
   - Solved: Running the epistemic parliament without an API key by spawning 3 parallel Task sub-agents
   - Solved: Parsing JSONL chat transcripts to extract embedded JSON
   - Solved: Assembly of full markdown paper from structured debate JSON
   - In progress: Factual corrections and content moderation via REPLACEMENTS list
   - Pending: HTML generation (script exists but HTML template not written)

6. All user messages:
   - Initial script paste: `pip install anthropic && python3 << 'EOF' ... ` (full Python script for Epistemic Parliament)
   - Answer to API key question: "just use your tokens"
   - Reminder system messages (ignored per instructions): task tools reminder
   - "continue" (after background task notification about aaf914e failing with usage limit)
   - Most recent: "1. check all facts ar ecorrect 2. format the paper so well its professional 3. remove anything that can get me cancelled 5. format like the ai repoublic and save it as html so i can make a domain"
   - Most recent: "Your response was cut off because it exceeded the output token limit. Please break your work into smaller pieces. Continue from where you left off."

7. Pending Tasks:
   - **Task #1**: Fact-correct paper claims (in_progress) — REPLACEMENTS list written but script not yet run
   - **Task #2**: Remove inflammatory content (in_progress) — included in REPLACEMENTS list, not yet applied
   - **Task #3**: Build premium HTML (in_progress) — NOT YET STARTED. Need to write HTML template with: serif body (Charter/Georgia), sans headings (Inter), role-colored agent blocks, cream background (#FAFAF5), burgundy/navy accents, editorial magazine layout

8. Current Work:
   Immediately before the summary request, I was building `/Users/samrajmatharu/build_html.py`. I had just finished writing the content-editing portion (REPLACEMENTS list and the loop that applies edits to all debate text, saving to `~/debate_results_edited.json`). The script ends with `print("Content edited. Now generating HTML...")` but the HTML generation portion was NOT written because the response hit the output token limit. The three TaskCreate tasks are all in_progress status. The design plan already established: premium editorial magazine style with serif body, sans headings, role-colored agent blocks (Advocate=navy #1E5F8E, Prosecutor=crimson #A83232, Methodologist=aubergine #4A3B76, Synthesiser=bronze #8B7355, Dissentor=burnt orange #C2702E, Historian=olive #556B2F), cream background #FAFAF5, dark text #1A1815.

9. Optional Next Step:
   Continue building the HTML. The user explicitly said "Please break your work into smaller pieces. Continue from where you left off." The direct next steps are:
   
   (a) Run the existing `build_html.py` to produce `~/debate_results_edited.json` with fact corrections and inflammatory content removal applied
   
   (b) Then write a separate Python script (or extend build_html.py incrementally) that generates the final HTML file at `~/epistemic_parliament.html`. This should be done in smaller pieces — e.g., first write the CSS + masthead + abstract sections, then the methodology section, then each question's debate section, then discussion/conclusion — to avoid hitting the output token limit again.
   
   The most recent assistant statement was: "I'll build the HTML via a Python script that reads the JSON data, applies the corrections, and generates the styled file in one pass." — and the script was created but only contains the corrections logic, not the HTML generation logic. The immediate next action should be running the existing script to apply the edits, then building HTML generation in chunks.
</summary>
